source_paper_title: Cardinal-Utility Matching Markets: The Quest for Envy-Freeness, Pareto-Optimality, and Efficient Computability
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Tr_bst_2025, title={Cardinal-Utility Matching Markets: The Quest for Envy-Freeness, Pareto-Optimality, and Efficient Computability}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0770}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0770}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Tröbst, Thorben and Vazirani, Vijay V.}, year={2025}, month=sept }
context:
Page 23, Section 4 (Conclusion)

Is there a polynomial time algorithm to find \(\alpha\)-approximately JEF+PO lotteries in two-sided markets, for any constant \(\alpha\)?
title: Polynomial-time approximation of justified envy-free Pareto-optimal lotteries in two-sided markets
background_latex:
Consider a bipartite two-sided cardinal-utility matching market with two sets of agents \(A\) and \(B\), with \(|A|=|B|=n\). Each \(i\in A\) has nonnegative rational utilities \(u_{ij}\) for being matched to \(j\in B\), and each \(j\in B\) has nonnegative rational utilities \(w_{ji}\) for being matched to \(i\in A\).

A (fractional) allocation is a matrix \(x=(x_{ij})_{i\in A, j\in B}\) with \(x_{ij}\ge 0\) satisfying the fractional perfect matching constraints
\[
\sum_{j\in B} x_{ij}=1 \ \forall i\in A, \qquad \sum_{i\in A} x_{ij}=1 \ \forall j\in B.
\]
This corresponds to a lottery over integral perfect matchings via Birkhoff--von Neumann decomposition, and agents evaluate allocations by expected utility.

Utilities induced by \(x\) are
\[
U_i(x)=\sum_{j\in B} u_{ij}x_{ij}\quad (i\in A), \qquad W_j(x)=\sum_{i\in A} w_{ji}x_{ij}\quad (j\in B).
\]

Justified envy-freeness (JEF) is a two-sided fairness notion. For \(i,i'\in A\), agent \(i\) has justified envy toward \(i'\) under allocation \(x\) if
\[
U_i(x) < \sum_{j\in B: \ w_{ji}\ge w_{ji'}} u_{ij} x_{i'j}.
\]
(The definition is symmetric for pairs \(j,j'\in B\).) An allocation \(x\) is JEF if it has no justified envy on either side.

Pareto-optimality (PO) in two-sided markets means there is no other feasible allocation \(y\) such that \(U_i(y)\ge U_i(x)\) for all \(i\in A\), \(W_j(y)\ge W_j(x)\) for all \(j\in B\), and at least one of these inequalities is strict.

For \(\alpha\ge 1\), an \(\alpha\)-approximately JEF allocation informally means justified envy is bounded within a multiplicative factor \(\alpha\) (the paper asks for an \(\alpha\)-approximation analogue of JEF, without fixing a particular formalization beyond the constant-factor approximation goal).
problem_definition_latex:
Design a polynomial-time algorithm that, given any bipartite two-sided cardinal-utility matching market \((A,B,u,w)\), outputs a feasible lottery/allocation \(x\) that is Pareto-optimal and \(\alpha\)-approximately justified envy-free for some constant \(\alpha\ge 1\) (independent of \(n\)).

Equivalently: determine whether there exists a constant \(\alpha\) for which such an allocation can always be computed in time polynomial in \(n\) and the input encoding size, and if so, give such an algorithm (including the achieved constant \(\alpha\)).
latex:
Consider a bipartite two-sided cardinal-utility matching market with two sets of agents \(A\) and \(B\), with \(|A|=|B|=n\). Each \(i\in A\) has nonnegative rational utilities \(u_{ij}\) for being matched to \(j\in B\), and each \(j\in B\) has nonnegative rational utilities \(w_{ji}\) for being matched to \(i\in A\).

A (fractional) allocation is a matrix \(x=(x_{ij})_{i\in A, j\in B}\) with \(x_{ij}\ge 0\) satisfying the fractional perfect matching constraints
\[
\sum_{j\in B} x_{ij}=1 \ \forall i\in A, \qquad \sum_{i\in A} x_{ij}=1 \ \forall j\in B.
\]
This corresponds to a lottery over integral perfect matchings via Birkhoff--von Neumann decomposition, and agents evaluate allocations by expected utility.

Utilities induced by \(x\) are
\[
U_i(x)=\sum_{j\in B} u_{ij}x_{ij}\quad (i\in A), \qquad W_j(x)=\sum_{i\in A} w_{ji}x_{ij}\quad (j\in B).
\]

Justified envy-freeness (JEF) is a two-sided fairness notion. For \(i,i'\in A\), agent \(i\) has justified envy toward \(i'\) under allocation \(x\) if
\[
U_i(x) < \sum_{j\in B: \ w_{ji}\ge w_{ji'}} u_{ij} x_{i'j}.
\]
(The definition is symmetric for pairs \(j,j'\in B\).) An allocation \(x\) is JEF if it has no justified envy on either side.

Pareto-optimality (PO) in two-sided markets means there is no other feasible allocation \(y\) such that \(U_i(y)\ge U_i(x)\) for all \(i\in A\), \(W_j(y)\ge W_j(x)\) for all \(j\in B\), and at least one of these inequalities is strict.

For \(\alpha\ge 1\), an \(\alpha\)-approximately JEF allocation informally means justified envy is bounded within a multiplicative factor \(\alpha\) (the paper asks for an \(\alpha\)-approximation analogue of JEF, without fixing a particular formalization beyond the constant-factor approximation goal).

Design a polynomial-time algorithm that, given any bipartite two-sided cardinal-utility matching market \((A,B,u,w)\), outputs a feasible lottery/allocation \(x\) that is Pareto-optimal and \(\alpha\)-approximately justified envy-free for some constant \(\alpha\ge 1\) (independent of \(n\)).

Equivalently: determine whether there exists a constant \(\alpha\) for which such an allocation can always be computed in time polynomial in \(n\) and the input encoding size, and if so, give such an algorithm (including the achieved constant \(\alpha\)).
open_problem_quote: Is there a polynomial time algorithm to find \(\alpha\)-approximately JEF+PO lotteries in two-sided markets, for any constant \(\alpha\)?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: If one fixes a concrete definition of \(\alpha\)-approximate JEF, small instances can be searched computationally (e.g., via LPs over the Birkhoff polytope) to test whether a candidate algorithm achieves a target \(\alpha\). However, the open question is existential/algorithmic rather than a crisp universal inequality, and negative results would require proving hardness or constructing families, not just a single small counterexample.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: There is a natural search space of candidate mechanisms (e.g., convex programs, regularized objectives, pricing/bargaining hybrids) with an automatic fitness signal: worst-case (or sampled) achieved JEF-approximation factor subject to PO, computed by solving LPs/convex programs on generated instances. The challenge is that guaranteeing PO and evaluating worst-case \(\alpha\) over all instances is hard, so the fitness signal would be heuristic (instance sampling, adversarial instance generation) rather than fully faithful.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
